tools.name: Anthropic API
tools.description: Anthropic's API for embedding Claude in your product. Sonnet 4.6 for quality, Haiku 4.5 for cost-efficient volume.
tools.solo_price: Claude Sonnet 4.6 ~$3/1M input, $15/1M output; Claude Haiku 4.5 ~$1/1M input, $5/1M output
tools.scale_ceiling: Rate limits increase with usage tier. Tier upgrades based on spend history.
tools.graduation_to: Optimize with prompt caching (up to 90% savings on repeated context), batching, or hybrid with open-source models
gotchas: Same unpredictability as OpenAI. Token costs scale with user activity, not seats.
Prompt caching can reduce costs 90% for repeated context. Use it aggressively.
Extended thinking adds cost. Only enable it for tasks that genuinely need reasoning.
Output tokens cost 5x more than input tokens. Design prompts for concise responses.
Rate limits are per-model. Spreading traffic across Sonnet and Haiku helps with throughput.

tools.name: Auth.js (NextAuth)
tools.description: Self-hosted auth for Next.js. Zero vendor lock-in. Zero per-user costs. You build the UI.
tools.scale_ceiling: None. Self-hosted scales with your infrastructure.
tools.graduation_to: N/A. Auth.js is the graduation destination for most.
gotchas: You build the auth UI yourself. No pre-built components.
More development time upfront: expect 1-2 weeks vs 1-2 days with Clerk
You're responsible for security best practices
Documentation has improved significantly but still has gaps
The investment pays off at scale. $0 forever is hard to argue with.

tools.name: Better-Auth
tools.description: Open-source, self-hosted auth library. Growing fast as the modern alternative to Auth.js.
tools.scale_ceiling: None. Self-hosted scales with your infrastructure.
tools.graduation_to: N/A. Self-hosted auth is the endgame.
gotchas: Newer project with a smaller community than Auth.js
Less battle-tested in production at large scale
Growing rapidly. Worth watching.
More modern API design than Auth.js

tools.name: BetterStack
tools.description: Uptime monitoring + logging in one platform. Formerly Better Uptime + Logtail.
tools.scale_ceiling: Free tier handles basic uptime monitoring. Pay when you need logging.
tools.graduation_to: Datadog or self-hosted Grafana stack
gotchas: Free tier is uptime-only. Logging requires paid plan.
Incident management features limited on free tier.

tools.name: Claude Code
tools.description: Anthropic's agentic coding tool. Available as CLI, desktop app, web app, and IDE extensions. Reads your entire codebase and executes multi-step tasks autonomously.
tools.free_tier: Included with Claude Pro ($20/mo) with usage limits
tools.solo_price: $20/mo (Pro plan) or pay-per-token via API (~$3-15/day for heavy agentic usage)
tools.scale_ceiling: None on API billing. Usage caps on Pro plan reset daily.
tools.graduation_to: API billing for unlimited agentic usage (no hard caps, just cost)

cost_at_scale.100_users: $20/mo Pro or variable API costs (per-developer tool)

gotchas: API billing can spike during heavy agentic sessions. A single complex task can cost $5-10 in tokens.
Pro plan has daily usage limits that reset. You will hit them during intense coding days.
Now available as CLI, desktop app (Mac/Windows), web app, and VS Code/JetBrains extensions.
Max mode uses more expensive models and burns through limits faster.
Per-developer tool. Cost scales with how hard you push it, not with your product's users.

tools.name: Clerk
tools.description: Drop-in auth with pre-built UI components. Fastest integration for Next.js. Beautiful out of the box.
tools.scale_ceiling: Free tier now covers most solo products. Costs kick in past 50K users.
tools.graduation_to: Auth.js or Better-Auth (self-hosted, $0 forever)
gotchas: Vendor lock-in is real. Pre-built components mean migrating away requires rebuilding auth UI.
No self-hosted option. Your auth depends on Clerk's infrastructure.
Free tier expanded to 50K in Feb 2026 (was 10K). Much more reasonable now.
The DX is genuinely excellent. Fastest path from zero to working auth.
Metric changed from MAU to MRU (monthly returning users). Verify how this affects your count.

tools.name: Cursor
tools.description: AI-native code editor built as a VS Code fork. Inline completions, chat, and agentic coding in one IDE.
tools.solo_price: $20/mo (Pro). Also Pro+ at $60/mo (3x usage) and Ultra at $200/mo (20x usage)
tools.scale_ceiling: Usage limits on Pro. Cursor no longer publishes specific request counts, uses relative multipliers instead.
tools.graduation_to: Claude Code for deeper agentic work, or API-based coding agents for unlimited usage
gotchas: Usage limits reset monthly. No rollover, no burst capacity.
Background agent burns through usage quickly. A single complex task can eat dozens of requests.
VS Code fork means some extensions may not work or may lag behind upstream updates.
Teams plan at $40/mo/seat (was Business) adds admin controls.
Per-developer cost, not per-user. Scales with team size, not product usage.

tools.name: GitHub Copilot
tools.description: AI pair programmer from GitHub. Best-in-class inline completions and chat, deeply integrated with VS Code and GitHub ecosystem.
tools.free_tier: Free plan with 2K completions + 50 chat messages/mo
tools.scale_ceiling: Premium request limits for advanced models. Free and Pro tiers have monthly caps.
tools.graduation_to: Cursor or Claude Code for heavier agentic refactoring work
gotchas: Best for completions and inline suggestions. Less capable for large refactors than Cursor or Claude Code.
Business plan now $30/mo/seat (was $19). Enterprise at $39/mo/seat.
Premium request limits gate access to more capable models like Claude and GPT-4o.
Free tier is generous for light usage but chat limit of 50 messages/mo is tight.
Per-developer cost. Scales with your team, not your product's user base.

tools.name: Hetzner + Coolify
tools.description: Self-hosted PaaS on cheap European VPS. Vercel-like DX (git push deploy, preview URLs, auto SSL) on infrastructure you own.
tools.free_tier: None (Hetzner starts at 3.99 EUR/mo, raised April 2026)
tools.scale_ceiling: You control the ceiling. Add more servers as needed.
tools.graduation_to: N/A. Self-hosted is the endgame.
gotchas: You manage the server. Coolify handles most DevOps but you're still responsible for uptime.
Hetzner datacenters are in Europe and US. Latency to Asia/Australia is higher.
Initial setup takes 1-2 hours vs 5 minutes on Vercel
No edge network. Static assets should go through a CDN (Cloudflare free tier).
The cost savings are dramatic: teams report 50-70% less than Vercel/Railway

tools.name: Highlight.io (now LaunchDarkly Observability)
tools.description: Session replay + error tracking + logging. Highlight.io was acquired by LaunchDarkly in Feb 2026. The product now lives under LaunchDarkly Observability.
tools.scale_ceiling: Free tier is much more generous than the old Highlight.io plans.
tools.graduation_to: Self-hosted (open source code still available) or Sentry + BetterStack
gotchas: Highlight.io as an independent product was deprecated Feb 2026. Migrated to LaunchDarkly.
The open source code is still available for self-hosting.
Pricing model changed significantly. Now under LaunchDarkly's usage-based pricing.

tools.name: Inngest
tools.description: Event-driven background jobs and workflows. Works with Vercel, Railway, any platform. No infrastructure to manage.
tools.scale_ceiling: Generous free tier covers most early products. Paid tier when runs increase.
tools.graduation_to: BullMQ + Redis (self-managed, $0)
gotchas: Vendor lock-in to Inngest's event model.
Free tier is 50K executions/mo. Runs add up with chatty events.
Debugging can be harder than local queue systems.

tools.name: Lemon Squeezy
tools.description: Merchant of Record. Handles all global tax compliance so you don't have to. Best for digital products sold globally.
tools.scale_ceiling: ~$50K MRR before the fee premium over Stripe becomes significant
tools.graduation_to: Stripe + Stripe Tax when the 2% fee difference matters
gotchas: Acquired by Stripe in late 2024. Still operating independently as of April 2026, but no major feature updates since acquisition. Widely expected to be folded into Stripe's own MoR offering.
Post-acquisition quality reports are mixed: some users report bugs and slower support, others say it's been stable. YMMV.
5% + $0.50 is nearly double Stripe's 2.9% + $0.30 at high volume
Less API flexibility than Stripe. Fewer integrations.
The upside is real though: zero thinking about VAT, sales tax, or compliance

tools.name: Neon
tools.description: Serverless Postgres that scales to zero. Pay only when your database is active.
tools.solo_price: Usage-based (Launch plan: ~$0.106/compute-hour + $0.35/GB-month storage, no fixed fee)
tools.scale_ceiling: Serverless means it scales up and down automatically.
tools.graduation_to: N/A for most use cases. Scales well.
gotchas: Cold starts on free tier can add 100-500ms to first query after inactivity
Switched to pure usage-based pricing in 2026. No more flat monthly tiers.
Free compute dropped from 190 to 100 hours/mo. Still enough for most MVPs.
No built-in auth, storage, or realtime. It's just the database.
Branching feature is excellent for development workflows
If you only need a database (not a full BaaS), Neon is a strong choice

tools.name: Open SaaS (Wasp)
tools.description: Free, open-source SaaS starter. No paid third-party dependencies. Full ownership from day one.
tools.scale_ceiling: N/A. It's a starting point.
tools.graduation_to: N/A. You own the code.
gotchas: Requires learning the Wasp framework (a DSL on top of React + Node)
Smaller community than ShipFast or Supastarter
10K+ GitHub stars suggest real adoption
The 'no vendor dependencies' philosophy means more DIY but total ownership
Good choice if you're philosophically opposed to paying for boilerplate

tools.name: OpenAI API
tools.description: OpenAI's API for embedding AI features in your product. GPT-4o for quality, GPT-4o-mini for cost-efficient volume.
tools.solo_price: GPT-4o ~$2.50/1M input, $10/1M output; GPT-4o-mini ~$0.15/1M input, $0.60/1M output
tools.scale_ceiling: Rate limits increase with usage tier. Tier upgrades are automatic based on spend history.
tools.graduation_to: Optimize with prompt caching, fine-tuning, or self-hosted open-source models for high-volume features
gotchas: Costs hard to predict without knowing your token usage patterns. Prototype first, then estimate.
Rate limits can block burst traffic. Plan for queuing or retries in your architecture.
No fixed monthly pricing. Your bill scales directly with user activity.
GPT-4o-mini is 15-20x cheaper than GPT-4o. Use the cheapest model that meets your quality bar.
Output tokens cost 4x more than input tokens. Keep responses concise to control costs.

tools.name: Paddle
tools.description: Merchant of Record with broader global tax coverage. More enterprise-oriented than Lemon Squeezy.
tools.scale_ceiling: Enterprise-ready. No practical ceiling.
tools.graduation_to: N/A. Paddle scales up. Some switch to Stripe for lower fees at high volume.
gotchas: Same 5% + $0.50 fee structure as Lemon Squeezy
Less indie-focused than Lemon Squeezy. Dashboard and DX feel more enterprise.
Chargeback dispute handling has been criticized by indie founders
Broader global tax coverage than Lemon Squeezy
Paddle Billing (subscription management) is solid but opinionated

tools.name: Plausible
tools.description: Simple, privacy-first web analytics. One dashboard, no complexity. The anti-Google Analytics.
tools.scale_ceiling: Scales fine. Pricing grows with pageviews.
tools.graduation_to: Add PostHog when you need product analytics (funnels, cohorts, session replay)
gotchas: No free tier. You pay from day one.
Website analytics only. No product analytics (funnels, cohorts, user paths).
If you need to understand user behavior inside your app, you need PostHog too.
The simplicity is the feature. One-minute setup, sub-500ms dashboard loads.
GDPR-compliant without cookie banners. EU-hosted option available.
Self-hosted option available (free, open source)

tools.name: PocketBase
tools.description: Single-binary backend with SQLite, auth, file storage, and admin UI. Self-hosted. Zero external dependencies.
tools.scale_ceiling: ~10K concurrent users. SQLite is the constraint.
tools.graduation_to: Supabase or dedicated Postgres when you need more concurrency
gotchas: SQLite-based. Single writer. Concurrent writes are the bottleneck.
No managed cloud option. You host it yourself.
Beloved by the 'own everything' crowd. One Go binary, zero dependencies.
Built-in admin UI is surprisingly good
Data migration to Postgres requires effort if you outgrow it

tools.name: PostHog
tools.description: Full product analytics suite. Analytics, session replay, feature flags, A/B tests, and surveys in one platform. Absurdly generous free tier.
tools.free_tier: 1M events/mo, 5K session replays, 1M feature flag requests
tools.scale_ceiling: None. Scales to enterprise.
tools.graduation_to: N/A. PostHog replaces what would cost $300+/mo on Mixpanel or Amplitude.
gotchas: Overkill for simple website analytics. If you just want pageview counts, use Plausible.
The dashboard can feel overwhelming at first. There's a lot of product in here.
Self-hosted option available for privacy-sensitive deployments
1M free events/mo means most solo SaaS products never pay
The combination of analytics + session replay + feature flags is genuinely unique at this price

tools.name: Postmark
tools.description: Transactional email with the best deliverability in the industry. Sub-2-hour support response times.
tools.scale_ceiling: Scales well. Deliverability stays excellent at volume.
tools.graduation_to: N/A. Postmark is the reliability choice at any scale.
gotchas: Free tier exists (100 emails/mo) but is really just for testing. You'll need paid for production.
More expensive per email than Resend at volume
The deliverability and support quality justify the premium for mission-critical email
Template system is solid but not React-based
If your emails absolutely must arrive in the inbox, not spam, this is the choice

tools.name: Railway
tools.description: Full-stack hosting in one place. Frontend, backend, database, and workers with predictable usage-based pricing.
tools.solo_price: $5/mo (Hobby, includes $5 usage credit) or ~$5-15/mo typical usage
tools.scale_ceiling: Scales well. No per-seat pricing gotchas.
tools.graduation_to: Hetzner + Coolify for maximum cost control
gotchas: Less edge optimization than Vercel. If you need global edge, Vercel or Cloudflare is better.
Usage-based pricing means costs are predictable but not fixed
Smaller community than Vercel. Fewer tutorials and guides.
Git push deploy, preview URLs, and managed databases all work well
Teams report 50-70% savings compared to Vercel at scale

tools.name: Resend
tools.description: Modern transactional email with React Email integration. The default choice for Next.js developers.
tools.scale_ceiling: Cost scales with volume. No major ceiling.
tools.graduation_to: Amazon SES ($0.10/1K emails) if volume gets extreme and you want raw cost savings
gotchas: No built-in template editor. You write email templates in code (React Email).
If you're not in the React/Next.js ecosystem, the DX advantage is smaller.
3K free emails/mo is enough for early stage but tight for apps with heavy email flows.
Developer experience is genuinely excellent. API is clean and well-documented.

tools.name: Sentry
tools.description: Error tracking and performance monitoring. The industry standard for catching bugs in production.
tools.scale_ceiling: Free tier covers most solo products. Team plan if you need more volume.
tools.graduation_to: Self-hosted Sentry (free, open source)
gotchas: Free tier is generous but has 24-hour data retention. Team plan has 90-day retention.
SDK can add bundle size if not tree-shaken.

tools.name: ShipFast
tools.description: Next.js SaaS boilerplate with active Discord community. Fastest path from idea to deployed product.
tools.scale_ceiling: N/A. It's a starting point, not a dependency.
tools.graduation_to: N/A. You own the code.
gotchas: Uses MongoDB by default. Not ideal for relational SaaS data. Can be changed.
The Discord community is a genuine advantage for getting unstuck fast
You still need to understand the code. Don't ship what you can't debug.
At $199 it saves you 1-2 weeks of boilerplate setup. That's the real value.
AI coding tools (Claude Code, Cursor) can scaffold similar setups in hours now

tools.name: Stripe
tools.description: Industry-standard payment processing. Maximum control and flexibility. You handle tax compliance.
tools.scale_ceiling: None. Stripe scales to any size.
tools.graduation_to: N/A. Stripe is the graduation destination.
gotchas: You are responsible for sales tax / VAT compliance in every jurisdiction you sell to
Tax compliance outside the US is non-trivial. Most solo founders underestimate this.
Stripe Tax add-on costs 0.5% per transaction on top of processing fees
Account can be frozen with funds held if Stripe flags your business as high-risk
Requires more integration work than Merchant of Record solutions

tools.name: Supabase Auth
tools.description: Auth included with Supabase. Postgres-backed. Cheapest per-user auth if you're already using Supabase.
tools.scale_ceiling: Feature-limited. No SSO, basic MFA, limited customization compared to Clerk.
tools.graduation_to: Clerk (if you need features) or Auth.js (if you need control)
gotchas: Tied to the Supabase ecosystem. If you leave Supabase DB, you likely leave Supabase Auth.
Auth UI components are less polished than Clerk's pre-built components
50K free MAU is extremely generous. Most solo SaaS never exceeds this.
RLS (Row Level Security) integration is powerful but has a learning curve
At 100K users, this costs $25/mo while Clerk costs $1,800/mo. That's not a typo.

tools.name: Supabase
tools.description: Postgres database + Auth + Storage + Realtime + Edge Functions in one platform. The default BaaS for solo founders.
tools.scale_ceiling: Handles most solo SaaS needs. Larger workloads may need dedicated Postgres.
tools.graduation_to: Dedicated Postgres (Neon, RDS, or self-hosted) if you outgrow the platform
gotchas: RLS (Row Level Security) is powerful but has a real learning curve
Vendor-specific features (Supabase Realtime, Storage) create soft lock-in
Free tier pauses after 1 week of inactivity. Not great for side projects you touch monthly.
The dashboard is excellent. SQL editor, table viewer, and auth management in one place.
Postgres underneath means your data is portable if you leave

tools.name: Supastarter
tools.description: Most feature-complete SaaS boilerplate. Multi-tenancy, i18n, monorepo architecture. For serious projects.
tools.scale_ceiling: N/A. It's a starting point.
tools.graduation_to: N/A. You own the code.
gotchas: Most expensive of the major boilerplates
Monorepo architecture has a learning curve if you're not familiar
The multi-tenancy and i18n support is genuinely hard to build from scratch
Available for both Next.js and Nuxt
Worth it if you're building a B2B SaaS with organization/team features

tools.name: Trigger.dev
tools.description: Open source background jobs for Next.js and Node.js. Long-running tasks, cron, and workflows.
tools.scale_ceiling: Free tier is small ($5 credit). Pro gives $50 usage credit and higher concurrency.
tools.graduation_to: Self-hosted Trigger.dev (open source) or BullMQ + Redis
gotchas: Free tier is just $5/mo credit. Can burn through quickly with active products.
Pricing is usage-based (compute seconds + invocation fees), not a simple bucket.
Open source so you can self-host to avoid per-run costs.
Newer project, smaller community than Inngest.

tools.name: Umami
tools.description: Privacy-first web analytics with a free tier. Like Plausible but with a free option.
tools.scale_ceiling: Scales fine. Similar trajectory to Plausible.
tools.graduation_to: Add PostHog for product analytics if needed
gotchas: Smaller ecosystem and community than Plausible or PostHog
100K free events/mo is generous for early-stage products
Self-hosted option available (open source)
A solid choice if you want Plausible-style simplicity with a free tier

tools.name: QStash (Upstash)
tools.description: Serverless message queue and task scheduler. Perfect for Vercel where you can't run persistent workers.
tools.scale_ceiling: Usage-based pricing stays cheap. No per-seat costs.
tools.graduation_to: BullMQ + Redis on Railway/Hetzner
gotchas: 1,000 messages/day on free tier is reasonable for most early products.
Serverless model means no persistent connections.
Best paired with Upstash Redis for state.

tools.name: Vercel
tools.description: Best-in-class Next.js hosting. Incredible DX with preview deploys and edge functions. Free tier has a bandwidth ceiling.
tools.scale_ceiling: ~33K pageviews/mo with images on free tier (100GB bandwidth). Pro adds more but per-seat pricing hurts teams.
tools.graduation_to: Railway (managed) or Hetzner + Coolify (self-hosted)
gotchas: 100GB bandwidth sounds like a lot until you serve images. A 3MB page load burns through it at ~33K views.
Each team member costs $20/mo on Pro. A team of 3 is $60/mo before bandwidth.
Serverless function cold starts can affect API response times
No built-in support for long-running background jobs
Vendor-specific features (Edge Config, KV) create soft lock-in
The DX is genuinely best-in-class. Hard to leave once you're used to it.

ai-tools.name: Anthropic API
ai-tools.description: Anthropic's API for embedding Claude in your product. Sonnet 4.6 for quality, Haiku 4.5 for cost-efficient volume.
ai-tools.solo_price: Claude Sonnet 4.6 ~$3/1M input, $15/1M output; Claude Haiku 4.5 ~$1/1M input, $5/1M output
ai-tools.scale_ceiling: Rate limits increase with usage tier. Tier upgrades based on spend history.
ai-tools.graduation_to: Optimize with prompt caching (up to 90% savings on repeated context), batching, or hybrid with open-source models
gotchas: Same unpredictability as OpenAI. Token costs scale with user activity, not seats.

ai-tools.description: AI-native code editor built as a VS Code fork. Inline completions, chat, and agentic coding in one IDE.
ai-tools.solo_price: $20/mo (Pro). Also Pro+ at $60/mo (3x usage) and Ultra at $200/mo (20x usage)
ai-tools.scale_ceiling: Usage limits on Pro. Cursor no longer publishes specific request counts, uses relative multipliers instead.
ai-tools.graduation_to: Claude Code for deeper agentic work, or API-based coding agents for unlimited usage

Business plan now $30/mo/seat (was $19). Enterprise at $39/mo/seat.
Premium request limits gate access to more capable models like Claude and GPT-4o.
Free tier is generous for light usage but chat limit of 50 messages/mo is tight.
Per-developer cost. Scales with your team, not your product's user base.

ai-tools.name: OpenAI API
ai-tools.description: OpenAI's API for embedding AI features in your product. GPT-4o for quality, GPT-4o-mini for cost-efficient volume.
ai-tools.solo_price: GPT-4o ~$2.50/1M input, $10/1M output; GPT-4o-mini ~$0.15/1M input, $0.60/1M output
ai-tools.scale_ceiling: Rate limits increase with usage tier. Tier upgrades are automatic based on spend history.
ai-tools.graduation_to: Optimize with prompt caching, fine-tuning, or self-hosted open-source models for high-volume features
gotchas: Costs hard to predict without knowing your token usage patterns. Prototype first, then estimate.
Rate limits can block burst traffic. Plan for queuing or retries in your architecture.
No fixed monthly pricing. Your bill scales directly with user activity.
GPT-4o-mini is 15-20x cheaper than GPT-4o. Use the cheapest model that meets your quality bar.

authentication.name: Auth.js (NextAuth)
authentication.description: Self-hosted auth for Next.js. Zero vendor lock-in. Zero per-user costs. You build the UI.
authentication.scale_ceiling: None. Self-hosted scales with your infrastructure.
authentication.graduation_to: N/A. Auth.js is the graduation destination for most.
gotchas: You build the auth UI yourself. No pre-built components.
More development time upfront: expect 1-2 weeks vs 1-2 days with Clerk
You're responsible for security best practices
Documentation has improved significantly but still has gaps
The investment pays off at scale. $0 forever is hard to argue with.

authentication.name: Better-Auth
authentication.description: Open-source, self-hosted auth library. Growing fast as the modern alternative to Auth.js.
authentication.scale_ceiling: None. Self-hosted scales with your infrastructure.
authentication.graduation_to: N/A. Self-hosted auth is the endgame.
gotchas: Newer project with a smaller community than Auth.js
Less battle-tested in production at large scale
Growing rapidly. Worth watching.
More modern API design than Auth.js

authentication.name: Clerk
authentication.description: Drop-in auth with pre-built UI components. Fastest integration for Next.js. Beautiful out of the box.
authentication.scale_ceiling: Free tier now covers most solo products. Costs kick in past 50K users.
gotchas: Vendor lock-in is real. Pre-built components mean migrating away requires rebuilding auth UI.
No self-hosted option. Your auth depends on Clerk's infrastructure.
Free tier expanded to 50K in Feb 2026 (was 10K). Much more reasonable now.
The DX is genuinely excellent. Fastest path from zero to working auth.
Metric changed from MAU to MRU (monthly returning users). Verify how this affects your count.

authentication.name: Supabase Auth
authentication.description: Auth included with Supabase. Postgres-backed. Cheapest per-user auth if you're already using Supabase.
authentication.scale_ceiling: Feature-limited. No SSO, basic MFA, limited customization compared to Clerk.
authentication.graduation_to: Clerk (if you need features) or Auth.js (if you need control)
gotchas: Tied to the Supabase ecosystem. If you leave Supabase DB, you likely leave Supabase Auth.
Auth UI components are less polished than Clerk's pre-built components
50K free MAU is extremely generous. Most solo SaaS never exceeds this.
RLS (Row Level Security) integration is powerful but has a learning curve
At 100K users, this costs $25/mo while Clerk costs $1,800/mo. That's not a typo.

monitoring.name: BetterStack
monitoring.description: Uptime monitoring + logging in one platform. Formerly Better Uptime + Logtail.
monitoring.scale_ceiling: Free tier handles basic uptime monitoring. Pay when you need logging.
gotchas: Free tier is uptime-only. Logging requires paid plan.
Incident management features limited on free tier.

monitoring.name: Highlight.io (now LaunchDarkly Observability)
monitoring.description: Session replay + error tracking + logging. Highlight.io was acquired by LaunchDarkly in Feb 2026. The product now lives under LaunchDarkly Observability.
monitoring.scale_ceiling: Free tier is much more generous than the old Highlight.io plans.
monitoring.graduation_to: Self-hosted (open source code still available) or Sentry + BetterStack
gotchas: Highlight.io as an independent product was deprecated Feb 2026. Migrated to LaunchDarkly.
The open source code is still available for self-hosting.
Pricing model changed significantly. Now under LaunchDarkly's usage-based pricing.

monitoring.name: Sentry
monitoring.description: Error tracking and performance monitoring. The industry standard for catching bugs in production.
monitoring.scale_ceiling: Free tier covers most solo products. Team plan if you need more volume.
gotchas: Free tier is generous but has 24-hour data retention. Team plan has 90-day retention.
SDK can add bundle size if not tree-shaken.

hosting.name: Hetzner + Coolify
hosting.description: Self-hosted PaaS on cheap European VPS. Vercel-like DX (git push deploy, preview URLs, auto SSL) on infrastructure you own.
hosting.free_tier: None (Hetzner starts at 3.99 EUR/mo, raised April 2026)
hosting.scale_ceiling: You control the ceiling. Add more servers as needed.
gotchas: You manage the server. Coolify handles most DevOps but you're still responsible for uptime.
Hetzner datacenters are in Europe and US. Latency to Asia/Australia is higher.
Initial setup takes 1-2 hours vs 5 minutes on Vercel
No edge network. Static assets should go through a CDN (Cloudflare free tier).
The cost savings are dramatic: teams report 50-70% less than Vercel/Railway

hosting.name: Railway
hosting.description: Full-stack hosting in one place. Frontend, backend, database, and workers with predictable usage-based pricing.
hosting.solo_price: $5/mo (Hobby, includes $5 usage credit) or ~$5-15/mo typical usage
hosting.scale_ceiling: Scales well. No per-seat pricing gotchas.
hosting.graduation_to: Hetzner + Coolify for maximum cost control
gotchas: Less edge optimization than Vercel. If you need global edge, Vercel or Cloudflare is better.
Usage-based pricing means costs are predictable but not fixed
Smaller community than Vercel. Fewer tutorials and guides.
Git push deploy, preview URLs, and managed databases all work well
Teams report 50-70% savings compared to Vercel at scale

hosting.name: Vercel
hosting.description: Best-in-class Next.js hosting. Incredible DX with preview deploys and edge functions. Free tier has a bandwidth ceiling.
hosting.scale_ceiling: ~33K pageviews/mo with images on free tier (100GB bandwidth). Pro adds more but per-seat pricing hurts teams.
gotchas: 100GB bandwidth sounds like a lot until you serve images. A 3MB page load burns through it at ~33K views.
Each team member costs $20/mo on Pro. A team of 3 is $60/mo before bandwidth.
Serverless function cold starts can affect API response times
No built-in support for long-running background jobs
Vendor-specific features (Edge Config, KV) create soft lock-in
The DX is genuinely best-in-class. Hard to leave once you're used to it.

background-jobs.name: Inngest
background-jobs.description: Event-driven background jobs and workflows. Works with Vercel, Railway, any platform. No infrastructure to manage.
background-jobs.scale_ceiling: Generous free tier covers most early products. Paid tier when runs increase.
gotchas: Vendor lock-in to Inngest's event model.
Free tier is 50K executions/mo. Runs add up with chatty events.
Debugging can be harder than local queue systems.

background-jobs.name: Trigger.dev
background-jobs.description: Open source background jobs for Next.js and Node.js. Long-running tasks, cron, and workflows.
background-jobs.scale_ceiling: Free tier is small ($5 credit). Pro gives $50 usage credit and higher concurrency.
background-jobs.graduation_to: Self-hosted Trigger.dev (open source) or BullMQ + Redis
gotchas: Free tier is just $5/mo credit. Can burn through quickly with active products.
Pricing is usage-based (compute seconds + invocation fees), not a simple bucket.
Open source so you can self-host to avoid per-run costs.
Newer project, smaller community than Inngest.

background-jobs.name: QStash (Upstash)
background-jobs.description: Serverless message queue and task scheduler. Perfect for Vercel where you can't run persistent workers.
background-jobs.scale_ceiling: Usage-based pricing stays cheap. No per-seat costs.
gotchas: 1,000 messages/day on free tier is reasonable for most early products.
Serverless model means no persistent connections.
Best paired with Upstash Redis for state.

payments.name: Lemon Squeezy
payments.description: Merchant of Record. Handles all global tax compliance so you don't have to. Best for digital products sold globally.
payments.scale_ceiling: ~$50K MRR before the fee premium over Stripe becomes significant
payments.graduation_to: Stripe + Stripe Tax when the 2% fee difference matters
gotchas: Acquired by Stripe in late 2024. Still operating independently as of April 2026, but no major feature updates since acquisition. Widely expected to be folded into Stripe's own MoR offering.
Post-acquisition quality reports are mixed: some users report bugs and slower support, others say it's been stable. YMMV.
5% + $0.50 is nearly double Stripe's 2.9% + $0.30 at high volume
Less API flexibility than Stripe. Fewer integrations.
The upside is real though: zero thinking about VAT, sales tax, or compliance

payments.name: Paddle
payments.description: Merchant of Record with broader global tax coverage. More enterprise-oriented than Lemon Squeezy.
payments.graduation_to: N/A. Paddle scales up. Some switch to Stripe for lower fees at high volume.
gotchas: Same 5% + $0.50 fee structure as Lemon Squeezy
Less indie-focused than Lemon Squeezy. Dashboard and DX feel more enterprise.
Chargeback dispute handling has been criticized by indie founders
Broader global tax coverage than Lemon Squeezy
Paddle Billing (subscription management) is solid but opinionated

payments.name: Stripe
payments.description: Industry-standard payment processing. Maximum control and flexibility. You handle tax compliance.
payments.scale_ceiling: None. Stripe scales to any size.
payments.graduation_to: N/A. Stripe is the graduation destination.
gotchas: You are responsible for sales tax / VAT compliance in every jurisdiction you sell to
Tax compliance outside the US is non-trivial. Most solo founders underestimate this.
Stripe Tax add-on costs 0.5% per transaction on top of processing fees
Account can be frozen with funds held if Stripe flags your business as high-risk
Requires more integration work than Merchant of Record solutions

database.name: Neon
database.description: Serverless Postgres that scales to zero. Pay only when your database is active.
database.solo_price: Usage-based (Launch plan: ~$0.106/compute-hour + $0.35/GB-month storage, no fixed fee)
database.scale_ceiling: Serverless means it scales up and down automatically.
database.graduation_to: N/A for most use cases. Scales well.
gotchas: Cold starts on free tier can add 100-500ms to first query after inactivity
Switched to pure usage-based pricing in 2026. No more flat monthly tiers.
Free compute dropped from 190 to 100 hours/mo. Still enough for most MVPs.
No built-in auth, storage, or realtime. It's just the database.
Branching feature is excellent for development workflows
If you only need a database (not a full BaaS), Neon is a strong choice

database.name: PocketBase
database.description: Single-binary backend with SQLite, auth, file storage, and admin UI. Self-hosted. Zero external dependencies.
database.scale_ceiling: ~10K concurrent users. SQLite is the constraint.
database.graduation_to: Supabase or dedicated Postgres when you need more concurrency
gotchas: SQLite-based. Single writer. Concurrent writes are the bottleneck.
No managed cloud option. You host it yourself.
Beloved by the 'own everything' crowd. One Go binary, zero dependencies.
Built-in admin UI is surprisingly good
Data migration to Postgres requires effort if you outgrow it

database.name: Supabase
database.description: Postgres database + Auth + Storage + Realtime + Edge Functions in one platform. The default BaaS for solo founders.
database.scale_ceiling: Handles most solo SaaS needs. Larger workloads may need dedicated Postgres.
database.graduation_to: Dedicated Postgres (Neon, RDS, or self-hosted) if you outgrow the platform
gotchas: RLS (Row Level Security) is powerful but has a real learning curve
Vendor-specific features (Supabase Realtime, Storage) create soft lock-in
Free tier pauses after 1 week of inactivity. Not great for side projects you touch monthly.
The dashboard is excellent. SQL editor, table viewer, and auth management in one place.
Postgres underneath means your data is portable if you leave

boilerplate.name: Open SaaS (Wasp)
boilerplate.description: Free, open-source SaaS starter. No paid third-party dependencies. Full ownership from day one.
gotchas: Requires learning the Wasp framework (a DSL on top of React + Node)
Smaller community than ShipFast or Supastarter
10K+ GitHub stars suggest real adoption
The 'no vendor dependencies' philosophy means more DIY but total ownership
Good choice if you're philosophically opposed to paying for boilerplate

boilerplate.name: ShipFast
boilerplate.description: Next.js SaaS boilerplate with active Discord community. Fastest path from idea to deployed product.
boilerplate.scale_ceiling: N/A. It's a starting point, not a dependency.
gotchas: Uses MongoDB by default. Not ideal for relational SaaS data. Can be changed.
The Discord community is a genuine advantage for getting unstuck fast
You still need to understand the code. Don't ship what you can't debug.
At $199 it saves you 1-2 weeks of boilerplate setup. That's the real value.
AI coding tools (Claude Code, Cursor) can scaffold similar setups in hours now

boilerplate.name: Supastarter
boilerplate.description: Most feature-complete SaaS boilerplate. Multi-tenancy, i18n, monorepo architecture. For serious projects.
gotchas: Most expensive of the major boilerplates
Monorepo architecture has a learning curve if you're not familiar
The multi-tenancy and i18n support is genuinely hard to build from scratch
Available for both Next.js and Nuxt
Worth it if you're building a B2B SaaS with organization/team features

analytics.name: Plausible
analytics.description: Simple, privacy-first web analytics. One dashboard, no complexity. The anti-Google Analytics.
analytics.scale_ceiling: Scales fine. Pricing grows with pageviews.
analytics.graduation_to: Add PostHog when you need product analytics (funnels, cohorts, session replay)
gotchas: No free tier. You pay from day one.
Website analytics only. No product analytics (funnels, cohorts, user paths).
If you need to understand user behavior inside your app, you need PostHog too.
The simplicity is the feature. One-minute setup, sub-500ms dashboard loads.
GDPR-compliant without cookie banners. EU-hosted option available.
Self-hosted option available (free, open source)

analytics.name: PostHog
analytics.description: Full product analytics suite. Analytics, session replay, feature flags, A/B tests, and surveys in one platform. Absurdly generous free tier.
analytics.free_tier: 1M events/mo, 5K session replays, 1M feature flag requests
analytics.graduation_to: N/A. PostHog replaces what would cost $300+/mo on Mixpanel or Amplitude.
gotchas: Overkill for simple website analytics. If you just want pageview counts, use Plausible.
The dashboard can feel overwhelming at first. There's a lot of product in here.
Self-hosted option available for privacy-sensitive deployments
1M free events/mo means most solo SaaS products never pay
The combination of analytics + session replay + feature flags is genuinely unique at this price

analytics.name: Umami
analytics.description: Privacy-first web analytics with a free tier. Like Plausible but with a free option.
analytics.scale_ceiling: Scales fine. Similar trajectory to Plausible.
analytics.graduation_to: Add PostHog for product analytics if needed
gotchas: Smaller ecosystem and community than Plausible or PostHog
100K free events/mo is generous for early-stage products
Self-hosted option available (open source)
A solid choice if you want Plausible-style simplicity with a free tier

email.name: Postmark
email.description: Transactional email with the best deliverability in the industry. Sub-2-hour support response times.
email.scale_ceiling: Scales well. Deliverability stays excellent at volume.
email.graduation_to: N/A. Postmark is the reliability choice at any scale.
gotchas: Free tier exists (100 emails/mo) but is really just for testing. You'll need paid for production.
More expensive per email than Resend at volume
The deliverability and support quality justify the premium for mission-critical email
Template system is solid but not React-based
If your emails absolutely must arrive in the inbox, not spam, this is the choice

email.name: Resend
email.description: Modern transactional email with React Email integration. The default choice for Next.js developers.
email.scale_ceiling: Cost scales with volume. No major ceiling.
email.graduation_to: Amazon SES ($0.10/1K emails) if volume gets extreme and you want raw cost savings
gotchas: No built-in template editor. You write email templates in code (React Email).
If you're not in the React/Next.js ecosystem, the DX advantage is smaller.
3K free emails/mo is enough for early stage but tight for apps with heavy email flows.
Developer experience is genuinely excellent. API is clean and well-documented.

stacks.name: The ~$50/month Production Stack
stacks.description: For launched products with paying customers. Optimized for solo founders making $500-5K MRR.
stacks.when_to_use: You have paying customers. You need reliability, better support, and room to grow.
stacks.when_to_graduate: When you need advanced auth features (SSO, org management), your infrastructure costs exceed $150/mo, or you want to own your hosting.

framework.why: Same framework. Don't change what works.

database.why: Pro plan removes the inactivity pause and adds daily backups. Essential for paying customers.

auth.limits: Unlimited MAU on Pro (you pay for Supabase, auth is included)
auth.why: Still the cheapest path. Auth is free as long as you're on Supabase.

payments.why: Handles all global tax compliance. At <$50K MRR, the fee premium over Stripe is worth the peace of mind.

hosting.why: Frontend + backend + database + workers in one place. No per-seat pricing surprises.

email.why: Upgraded for higher volume. Still great DX.

analytics.why: Free tier still covers most solo products at this stage.

stacks.name: The ~$100/month Scale Stack
stacks.description: For products with 10K+ users approaching product-market fit. Optimized for cost control and zero per-user cliffs.
stacks.when_to_use: You have product-market fit. You want to own your infrastructure and eliminate per-user pricing that scales against you.
stacks.when_to_graduate: You probably don't need to. This stack handles most solo SaaS products through $50K+ MRR.

framework.why: Same framework. Consistency is a feature.

database.why: Supabase Pro handles most solo SaaS workloads. Switch to dedicated Postgres only if you hit real limits.

auth.why: Self-managed auth costs $0 forever. At 100K users, this saves $1,800/mo vs Clerk. The migration takes effort but eliminates the biggest cost cliff in the stack.

payments.why: At $50K+ MRR, the 2% savings over Lemon Squeezy covers the Stripe Tax cost and then some.

hosting.why: Vercel-like DX on infrastructure you own. 50-70% cheaper than managed platforms.

email.why: Best deliverability. When your customers are paying, their emails must arrive.

analytics.why: Still the best value. Session replay alone is worth it for debugging customer issues.

monitoring.why: Error tracking for production. Free tier is enough for most solo products.

stacks.name: The $0/month MVP Stack
stacks.description: Ship your first version without spending a dollar. Every component has a free tier generous enough for your first 100 users.
stacks.when_to_use: Validating an idea. Pre-revenue. Getting your first 100 users. You have more time than money.
stacks.when_to_graduate: When you hit Vercel's 100GB bandwidth cap (~33K pageviews/mo with images), need team features, or want better support SLAs.

framework.why: Most AI training data, largest ecosystem, one language front-to-back

database.why: Postgres + Auth + Storage + Realtime in one platform. Free tier covers most MVPs.

auth.why: Already included. No additional service to manage.

payments.why: Industry standard. At low volume, the fee difference vs MoR platforms is negligible.

hosting.why: Best Next.js DX. Git push to deploy.

email.why: Clean API, React Email integration. 3K emails is enough for early stage.

analytics.why: Product analytics + session replay + feature flags. All free. Overkill in the best way.
